title: Utilization of Mahalanobis Distance on Motor Imagery EEG Channel Selection
authors: Seungjun Lee
abstract: In this study, a Mahalanobis distance based common spatial pattern (CSP)model (MD-CSP) was proposed and its performance applied to brain-computer interface (BCI) and motor imagery EEG signal processing was evaluated. EEG signals are often contaminated by noise and artifacts, which can degrade classification performance. This study introduces a Mahalanobis distance-based approach for noise rejection and optimal EEG channel selection, improving signal quality for brain-computer interface (BCI) applications. The experiment was conducted using the BCI competition IV 2a dataset and compared with CSPRank, L1 Norm of CSP, SCSPrank, and E-CSP. As a result of the experiment, MD-CSP performed particularly well at low number of channels and recorded an average accuracy of 60% in the two-channel configuration, outperforming 30%-43% accuracy in established models. MD-CSP showed better accuracy compared to other existing methods and showed a particularly noticeable difference in performance for less than five channels. This study shows that MD-CSP is effective in channel selection and noise removal in BCI system and suggests the possibility of application to real-time portable BCI system in the future.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; invasive/high-bandwidth BCI; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: SSVEP EEG signal recognition based on spiking neural network
authors: Gang Wang, Kaiming Sun, Da Zhou, Jianping Zhang, Lixin Lin, Lili Zhou
venue: Conference on Computer Graphics, Artificial Intelligence, and Data Processing
abstract: This paper proposes a steady-state visual evoked potential electroencephalogram signal recognition method based on a spiking neural network, aiming to advance brain-computer interface and brain-like artificial intelligence technologies. Through in-depth investigation of SSVEP signal characteristics and SNN mechanisms, a novel network architecture is designed for EEG signal decoding. The study utilizes a non-invasive EEG cap to acquire multi-channel signals, which undergo band-pass filtering preprocessing. The network adopts a fully connected structure and is trained using the STAC learning algorithm, achieving stable convergence within 400 training epochs. Experiments involving five subjects collected SSVEP signals evoked by visual stimuli of 40 different characters, with datasets divided into training and testing sets in a 9:1 ratio. Results demonstrate that the system achieves an average recognition accuracy of 97.85% in the 40-class classification task, with all subjects maintaining accuracy above 96.6%, validating the method's advantages in recognition precision and computational efficiency. This research provides a new approach for developing efficient real-time BCI systems and holds application value in the fields of neural signal decoding and neuro engineering.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; clinical/rehab relevance; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Ordinal pattern of brain electrical activity as a marker of stroke-induced alterations in motor imagery task.
authors: Aditi Bose, Pragati Gupta, K. Vemuri, Dhiman Das, Dibakar Ghosh, Syamal Kumar Dana, et al.
abstract: While the multidimensional features of electroencephalographic (EEG) signals have proven to be a valuable source of information, the development of a comprehensive diagnostic tool remains elusive due to variability of responses as observed within the subjects and epochs. We investigate whether ordinal-pattern-based complexity measures of EEG signals can capture stroke-related alterations in motor imagery (MI) tasks. EEG recordings from 36 stroke patients (acute and minor) and 36 healthy controls were analyzed using permutation entropy (PE), a robust symbolic measure of temporal irregularity. Stroke patients perform left- and right-hand MI tasks, while controls are recorded only under eye-open MI and eye-closed resting conditions. Results show that resting-state EEG from healthy participants exhibits low PE values, reflecting structured and regular dynamics, whereas eye-open MI EEG from the same cohort produces high PE values consistent with near-maximally complex, information-rich neural dynamics. Stroke patients demonstrate intermediate PE values during MI tasks, suggesting altered but partially preserved physiological complexity. These findings indicate that entropy-based measures can distinguish between healthy and stroke-related neural dynamics, providing potential biomarkers for tailoring brain-computer interface (BCI) driven rehabilitation strategies.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; invasive/high-bandwidth BCI; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Neural Digital Twins: Toward Next-Generation Brain-Computer Interfaces
authors: Mohammad Mahdi Habibi Bina, Sepideh Baghernezhad, M. Daliri, M. Moradi
abstract: Current neural interfaces such as brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) face several fundamental challenges, including frequent recalibration due to neuroplasticity and session-to-session variability, real-time processing latency, limited personalization and generalization across subjects, hardware constraints, surgical risks in invasive systems, and cognitive burden in patients with neurological impairments. These limitations significantly affect the accuracy, stability, and long-term usability of BCIs. This article introduces the concept of the Neural Digital Twin (NDT) as an advanced solution to overcome these barriers. NDT represents a dynamic, personalized computational model of the brain-BCI system that is continuously updated with real-time neural data, enabling prediction of brain states, optimization of control commands, and adaptive tuning of decoding algorithms. The design of NDT draws inspiration from the application of Digital Twin technology in advanced industries such as aerospace and autonomous vehicles, and leverages recent advances in artificial intelligence and neuroscience data acquisition technologies. In this work, we discuss the structure and implementation of NDT and explore its potential applications in next-generation BCIs and neural decoding, highlighting its ability to enhance precision, robustness, and individualized control in neurotechnology.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Mamba2-CNN Hybrid Model: A Novel Paradigm for Motor Imagery EEG Decoding
authors: Xin Xia, Lin Lei
venue: International journal of pattern recognition and artificial intelligence
abstract: Brain computer interface (BCI) systems translate noisy electroencephalogram (EEG) signals into control commands, with motor imagery (MI) classification being a central research focus. However, MI EEG decoding remains challenging due to the non-stationary nature of brain signals and their complex spatiotemporal patterns. This paper presents Mamba2-CNN, a hybrid architecture that integrates convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with the Mamba2 module based on the Structured State Space Duality (SSD) framework. The CNN component captures local spatial–temporal EEG patterns, while the Mamba2 backbone models long-range temporal dependencies with linear computational complexity, addressing the limitations of purely convolutional models. Comprehensive evaluations on BCI Competition IV-2a, IV-2b, and SEED, demonstrate that Mamba2-CNN achieves consistently competitive or state-of-the-art performance across both MI and emotion recognition tasks. Additional ablation and efficiency analyses indicate that the proposed model maintains a favorable balance between accuracy, latency, and memory usage, highlighting its practical relevance for real-time and resource-constrained BCI applications.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: MSHCNet: A Multi-Scale Hierarchical Complementary Network for EEG-Based Motor Imagery Classification
authors: Jun Yang, Jiahao Zhang, Tao Shen, Fanan Chen
venue: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: By deciphering electroencephalography (EEG) signals, the brain-computer interface (BCI) allows for direct interaction with external devices. However, the low spatial resolution, strong noise interference, and non-stationarity of EEG signals severely limit decoding performance. To address this issue, this paper proposes a model named MSHCNet, based on hierarchical feature extraction and a multi-scale complementary architecture, for multi-scale modeling of non-stationary EEG signals. The model employs spatio-temporal convolution to extract local features, while integrating sliding windows, multi-head self-attention, and temporal convolutional networks to capture long-range dependencies, thereby extracting global temporal features and generating sliding-window features through multi-window integration. Subsequently, a fusion module is designed to integrate local and global features, and an adaptive complementary architecture is introduced to fuse the global and sliding-window features, enhancing the model’s robustness to individual differences and noise. Experiments on the BCI Competition IV 2a dataset show that MSHCNet achieves an accuracy of 80.75% for motor imagery classification, and under few-shot fine-tuning conditions, it attains a cross-subject recognition accuracy of 65.02%, demonstrating its effectiveness and potential in complex EEG pattern recognition.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; invasive/high-bandwidth BCI; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Frequency-dependent filter lengths to decrease latency in motor ECoG Brain-Computer Interfaces
authors: Mathis Etienne, Félix Martel, T. Aksenova, Stéphane Bonnet
abstract: A major challenge in motor Brain-Computer Interfaces relies in achieving highly accurate decoding of motor intent with a minimal delay between the onset of the user’s neural activity and the resulting movement of the effector, while keeping the required computational power relatively low. In this study, we propose to design a complex Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) feature extraction, which uses variable filter lengths depending on the filtered frequencies. This approach offers two key benefits: (i) directly reducing the features delay inherent to online signal-processing pipelines, especially in the high-frequencies, and, as a consequence, (ii) a time-wise resynchronization of the two signatures associated with motor imagery/execution: the Event-Related Synchronization (ERS) in the high-frequencies and the Event-Related Desynchronization (ERD) in the low-frequencies. Simulation using several months of three-state decoding data recorded with two paraplegic participants implanted with a long-term electrocorticography device have been conducted. Results showed that this approach not only resulted in a decrease in decoding latencies, but also in an increase in decoding accuracy.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Feature-Space Conditional Data Augmentation for Motor Imagery EEG Classification
authors: Zihan Chen, Yi-Feng Zhang, Jiayao Hu, Yang Yu, Ling-Li Zeng
venue: International Conference on the Software Process
abstract: Motor imagery (MI) electroencephalogram (EEG) classification is often constrained by limited training samples, which restrict the discriminative capability and generalization performance of learning models. To overcome this, we propose a feature-level data augmentation framework based on Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGAN). Unlike conventional methods, this approach executes data generation directly within a discriminative feature space constructed by the Filter Bank Common Spatial Pattern with Temporal Structure Preservation (FBCSP-TS), which enhances class separability while retaining temporal dynamics. Within this compact representation space, a class-conditional CGAN is employed to model the underlying feature distributions and synthesize high-fidelity, category-consistent samples for training. Experiments on the primary BCI-IV-2a dataset demonstrate that the proposed feature-level framework significantly improves classification performance over unaugmented baselines (achieving a peak accuracy of 65.73% for CNN and an absolute improvement of up to 6.29% for SVM), and outperforms traditional signal-level strategies. Additionally, its broader effectiveness is confirmed on the supplementary BCI-III-IVa dataset. Furthermore, ablation experiments and parameter evaluations confirm that explicit conditional guidance and optimally calibrated augmentation scales are crucial for achieving stable and discriminative feature generation. This study demonstrates that performing generation within a task-relevant feature space provides a more robust and effective augmentation paradigm, offering practical insights for data-efficient brain-computer interface (BCI) systems.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Entropy-Based Dual-Teacher Distillation for Efficient Motor Imagery EEG Classification
authors: Zefeng Xu, Zhu Liang Yu
abstract: Motor imagery (MI) EEG classification is a key component of noninvasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) and often must satisfy strict latency constraints in online or edge deployments. Although ensembling can reliably improve MI decoding accuracy, its inference cost grows linearly with the number of ensemble members, making it impractical for low-latency applications. To address these issues, we propose an entropy-based dual-teacher distillation framework that transfers ensemble teacher knowledge to a single deployable backbone. From an information theoretic perspective, two failure modes are common in small and noisy MI datasets: elevated predictive entropy (noisy decisions) and large fluctuation across late training epochs (unstable convergence and unreliable checkpoint selection). Thus, we introduce an exponential moving average (EMA) teacher with entropy-gated activation as a low-pass filter in parameter space to reduce the student's prediction noise. In addition, a two-stage cosine annealing schedule is employed to suppress late-stage oscillations and improve the robustness of final checkpoint selection. Experiments on two public MI benchmarks (BCI Competition IV-2a and IV-2b) with three representative backbones (EEGNet, ShallowConvNet, and ATCNet) under the subject dependent protocol show consistent accuracy gains over the ensemble teacher and strong distillation baselines. On IV-2a, our method achieves an average accuracy of 0.7713 across the backbones, surpassing both the original models (0.7222) and the corresponding ensembles (0.7482); on IV-2b, it achieves 0.8583 versus 0.8432 (original) and 0.8529 (ensemble).

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; clinical/rehab relevance; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: EEG Signal Denoising and Feature Extraction for Seizure Detection and Motor Imagery
authors: R. Gayathri, Thenmozhi. B, S. B, Vishalini. K
venue: International Conference Intelligent Computing and Control Systems
abstract: Electroencephalogram signals are widely applied in medical diagnosis and Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) system, due to that they can reveal the abundant information of human brain activities. Nevertheless, these signals are too weak, and are prone to be interfered by artifacts including blink, muscle motion, environment electrical noise etc. Conventional denoising techniques, such as the Cascaded Thinning in Upscale and Downscale Representation (CTUDR) algorithm, are efficient but are based on fixed structuring elements. This restriction means they are less suitable if the EEG frequency or signal features vary during recording. The work proposes a novel Adaptive Morphological Structuring (AMS) technique. The size and shape of the structuring element are dynamically adapted in the proposed AMS method based on the frequency information of each EEG segment. It has the advantage of filtering both low- and high-frequency noise while preserving the signal features without affecting significant details of the signal. Graph-Based Skeleton Optimization (GSO) as a second step further enhances the denoised result. It eliminates tiny, undesirable branches generated by residual noise and preserves a concise and coherent waveform structure. The system calculates scalar features including energy, entropy, and band power, enabling improved accuracy of seizure with 96.68% and motorimagery with 94.92%. Furthermore, its real-time capability makes it suitable for deployment in continuous monitoring systems and clinical decision-support for neurological disorder management.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: A Robust Motor Imagery EEG Classification Framework Using Adaptive Channel–Window Selection and Temporal Feature Pooling
authors: Marwan Hilali, Kais Belwafi, Ghaffari Fakhreddine
venue: IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Motor Imagery (MI)-based Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) rely on accurate decoding of Electroencephalography (EEG) activity to enable intuitive communication and control. Because EEG signals naturally exhibit temporal evolution and inter-subject variability, effective models must accommodate these intrinsic properties rather than view them as limitations. In this work, we propose a robust classification framework that integrates Adaptive Channel and Window Selection (ACWS), Common Spatial Pattern (CSP), and a lightweight temporal pooling strategy inspired by sequence-encoding models. The ACWS module automatically identifies subject-specific electrodes and temporal segments that maximize the discriminability of MI activity, while CSP extracts spatial patterns. Temporal dynamics are further summarized using channel-wise pooling and positional encoding, with features compacted through Principle Component Analysis (PCA). These representations are classified using an XGBoost model, with the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) applied to address class imbalance. The proposed framework was validated on a publicly available dataset comprising EEG recordings from 62 subjects performing four MI tasks. Performance was evaluated using both intra-subject 5-fold cross-validation and a leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol to assess inter-subject generalization. The system achieved average accuracies of 85.03% for the binary case (left vs. right hand) and 80.10% for the four-class scenario, with an inter-subject standard deviation below 3.2%. These results surpass several CSP, Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based baselines tested under the same conditions, while maintaining consistent performance across participants.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; modern ML method; BCI term matches=2; title BCI matches=2; open PDF metadata
title: Predicting Brain Computer Interface Gaming Performance Using Motor Imagery Electroencephalography and Deep Learning
authors: Lenardo Kulesza, Ademir Verona Luz
abstract: Early estimation of user capability remains a central requirement for adaptive brain computer interface gaming systems and directly affects usability, engagement, and training efficiency. Our research addresses the problem of forecasting multi-level control performance before gameplay by analyzing pre task motor imagery electroencephalography recorded with consumer grade devices. The scope focuses on supervised performance prediction across low medium and high skill groups derived from objective three dimensional gameplay scores. Time frequency representations derived from preprocessed neural signals were learned by a compact deep learning classifier trained on balanced datasets from two independent headsets. Evidence was drawn from epoch level evaluation and cross subject validation with subject wise voting, supported by statistical confidence analysis and visualization of learned spectral patterns. The results show high discrimination accuracy at the sample level and stable generalization across unseen participants, with higher performance observed for devices offering improved spatial sampling. Findings indicate early motor imagery responses encode information linked to later control proficiency. The contribution extends prior motor imagery decoding work toward predictive user profiling based on task related neural activity rather than command accuracy. Practical implications include personalized difficulty adaptation and reduced calibration effort. Future work should examine larger cohorts, hybrid spectral spatial representations, transfer learning across hardware, and real time deployment within adaptive neurogaming environments.

title: Improving Individual-Specific SSVEP-BCI with Adaptive Channel and Subspace Selection in TRCA
authors: Hui Li, Guanghua Xu, Shanzheng Feng, Chenghang Du, Chengcheng Han, Jiachen Kuang, et al.
abstract: The individual-specific steady-state visual evoked potential (SSVEP)-based brain–computer interface (BCI) is characterized by individual calibration data, resulting in satisfactory performance. However, existing individual-specific SSVEP-BCIs employ generalized channels and task-related subspaces, which seriously limit their potential advantages and lead to suboptimal solutions. In this study, AS-TRCA was proposed to develop a purely individual-specific SSVEP-BCI by fully exploiting individual-specific knowledge. AS-TRCA involves optimal channel learning and selection (OCLS) as well as optimal subspace selection (OSS). OCLS aims to pick the optimal subject-specific channels by employing sparse learning with spatial distance constraints. Meanwhile, OSS adaptively determines the appropriate number of optimal subject-specific task-related subspaces by maximizing profile likelihood. The extensive experimental results demonstrate that AS-TRCA can acquire meaningful channels and determine the proper number of task-related subspaces for each subject compared to traditional methods. Furthermore, combining AS-TRCA with existing advanced calibration-based SSVEP decoding methods, including deep learning methods, to establish a purely individual-specific SSVEP-BCI can further enhance the decoding performance of these methods. Specifically, AS-TRCA improved the average accuracy as follows: TRCA 7.21%, SSCOR 7.61%, TRCA-R 6.58%, msTRCA 7.70%, scTRCA 4.47%, TDCA 2.91%, and bi-SiamCA 3.23%. AS-TRCA is promising for further advancing the performance of SSVEP-BCI and promoting its practical applications.
